description: Non-latency metadata for every hosting provider evaluated in the MCP server latency benchmark. Covers providers that are in the benchmark, providers not yet added, and providers explicitly excluded. Updated manually as new providers are evaluated.

benchmark_contract.measurement: Each pinger sends a single stateless JSON-RPC POST with method 'tools/list' and id 1, with headers 'Content-Type: application/json' and 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream'. No prior 'initialize' call, no session id, no warm-up, no retry. The measured latency is the wall-clock time between the send and the complete response.
benchmark_contract.identical_server: The same MCP server source code is deployed unchanged on each provider (same tools, same handlers). Latency differences reflect hosting infrastructure only, not server logic.
benchmark_contract.cadence: Every 30 minutes in relay — 12 geographic origins across 6 continents, each origin pings every 6 hours.

criteria_explained.sources: List of URLs where the information about this provider was found. Each entry includes the URL, the date it was consulted (checked_on), and a short description of what was found there. The checked_on date allows readers to verify whether the information has changed since it was recorded.
criteria_explained.benchmark_status: Whether this provider is currently measured in the benchmark ('in_benchmark'), evaluated but not yet deployed ('pending'), or excluded with a reason ('excluded').
criteria_explained.free_usage_permanent: true if the provider offers free usage with no time limit. A trial (limited days or credits) is false. This is the key distinction: a developer can use the provider indefinitely for free within usage limits.
criteria_explained.no_credit_card: true if the provider allows account creation and server deployment without ever entering a credit card. false means a CC is required at some point before a server can run. null means not yet verified.
criteria_explained.agent_deployable_100pct: true if an agent (or developer) can deploy and redeploy a server entirely from the terminal, without any manual action on a web interface. false means at least one step requires clicking in a browser (e.g. web-only code editor, dashboard-only initial setup). null means not yet verified.
criteria_explained.credit_card_required: Whether a credit card is required to create an account and deploy a server. 'account_only' means no CC needed to create the account but CC is needed to deploy. 'yes' means CC is required at some point before a server can run.
criteria_explained.agent_native_deployment: Whether the full deployment process (excluding account creation) can be performed by an agent from the terminal, without any manual action on a web interface. 'yes' means a CLI covers everything. 'partial' means some steps require browser interaction. 'no' means deployment requires the browser.
criteria_explained.free_tier_type: Type of free offering: 'permanent' (free forever within usage limits), 'trial' (free for a limited time or resource budget, then requires payment), 'none' (no free option).
criteria_explained.free_tier_limit: The specific limit of the free offering, if any.
criteria_explained.agent_native_score: Overall assessment of how well this provider fits an agent-native workflow where an agent handles deployment end-to-end without human intervention or payment.
criteria_explained.zero_to_live_sequence: Ordered list of steps to go from zero to a live MCP server endpoint. Each step specifies whether it requires a browser (manual) or can be done via CLI (agent-executable). This documents the real adoption cost for a developer or agent starting from scratch.
criteria_explained.execution_model: How the hosted MCP server actually runs. 'single-region' = one datacenter handles every request. 'edge-network' = requests run at the edge location nearest to each client, typically with a function origin region for serverless code. 'anycast' = the same code runs simultaneously in many datacenters worldwide, all reachable via one IP; every client hits the nearest one.
criteria_explained.deployment_region: City + country of the datacenter that actually executes the MCP server code for this deployment. Set to null when execution_model is 'anycast' or 'edge-network' without a single origin. For 'edge-network' with a declared function origin, this is that origin region.
criteria_explained.internal_name: Optional override for the internal key used in mcp_servers_under_test.json (the file listing the live MCP endpoints pinged by the benchmark). Set when the display name does not match the key, for example 'Val Town' is stored as 'valtown'. When present, the publisher uses this value to match measurement records to metadata.

providers.name: Cloudflare Workers
providers.region_source: Cloudflare Workers docs — Workers run at every Cloudflare data center simultaneously via anycast. There is no single deployment region.
providers.deployment_cli: wrangler (via npx, no global install required)
providers.agent_native_score: excellent — no CC, permanent free tier, full CLI deployment

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Scaffold and deploy: npm create cloudflare@latest then npx wrangler deploy

sources.content: Free tier limits: 100,000 requests per day
Onboarding sequence: npm create cloudflare → npx wrangler deploy. No CC required.

providers.name: Vercel
providers.region_source: Response header x-vercel-id on 2026-04-20 had the form 'cdg1::lhr1::<request_id>'. Vercel documents this header as '<edge_pop>::<function_region>::<request_id>'. The function region lhr1 is Vercel's London region, which is the serverless origin for this deployment. Edge routing fans out globally in front of that origin.
providers.free_tier_limit: unknown — request limits not publicly documented in detail
providers.agent_native_score: excellent — no CC, permanent free tier, full CLI deployment, native Claude Code plugin available

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a Vercel account (GitHub OAuth accepted)
vercel login (opens browser once for OAuth)

sources.content: Onboarding: vercel login then vercel --prod. No CC required. Claude Code plugin available.

providers.name: Netlify
providers.region_source: Netlify Functions run on AWS Lambda and default to the us-east-1 region (Northern Virginia, Ashburn) unless a region is explicitly configured in netlify.toml. The deployment used in this benchmark does not override the default. Netlify's CDN edge then fans out globally in front of that origin.
providers.agent_native_score: good — no CC, permanent free tier, full CLI deployment

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a Netlify account (GitHub OAuth accepted)
netlify login (opens browser once for OAuth)

sources.content: Free tier: 125k serverless function invocations per month
Onboarding: netlify login then netlify deploy. No CC required.

providers.name: Railway
providers.region_source: Response header x-railway-edge on 2026-04-20 was 'railway/europe-west4-drams3a'. europe-west4 is the Google Cloud Platform region in Eemshaven, Netherlands, which Railway uses for this deployment. Railway does front the container with a Fastly POP for TLS termination (x-served-by header), but the container itself runs in a single region.
providers.free_tier_limit: $5 of credits, expires in 7 days or when credits run out — whichever comes first
providers.agent_native_score: good — full CLI deployment, CC requirement to be verified

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a Railway account (GitHub OAuth accepted)
railway login (opens browser once for OAuth)

sources.content: Onboarding: railway init then railway up. CC requirement not mentioned in quickstart.
Trial expires in 7 days or when credits run out. App paused if not upgraded. Confirms free_tier_type = trial, not permanent.

providers.notes: free_tier_type corrected from 'permanent' to 'trial' on 2026-04-19 based on Railway email: trial expires in 7 days or when $5 credits run out. CC requirement still not verified.

providers.name: Supabase Edge Functions
providers.region_source: Supabase Edge Functions run on Deno Deploy. Deno Deploy is a global edge runtime: each request executes in the data center nearest to the client. Response header x-sb-edge-region on 2026-04-20 reflected the edge that served the request, not a fixed deployment region. The Supabase project region (us-east-1 Virginia, from 'supabase projects list') is where the project's database lives, not where Edge Functions execute.
providers.agent_native_score: good — full CLI deployment, CC requirement to be verified

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a Supabase account (GitHub OAuth accepted)
supabase login (opens browser once for OAuth)

sources.content: Free tier: 500,000 Edge Function invocations per month
Onboarding: supabase init → login → link → functions deploy. CC requirement not mentioned.

providers.notes: CC requirement not yet verified. To be confirmed at account creation.

providers.name: Fermyon
providers.region_source: Fermyon Cloud (the free-tier hosting platform used here) currently offers a single deployment region in Northern Virginia (Ashburn). All applications on Fermyon Cloud run from that region.
providers.credit_card_required: no — Starter tier is free and requires no credit card, only a GitHub account
providers.free_tier_limit: unknown — Starter tier documented as free, specific request limits not found
providers.agent_native_score: excellent — no CC, only GitHub account required, permanent free tier, full CLI deployment

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a GitHub account if not already done
spin login (opens browser once, authenticates via GitHub)

sources.content: No CC required. Starter tier is free. Only a GitHub account is needed. Full CLI sequence: spin new → spin build → spin login → spin deploy.

providers.notes: Fermyon is the only provider in the benchmark that requires neither a credit card nor a dedicated account — just a GitHub account. This makes it the most accessible for agent-native workflows.

providers.name: Val Town
providers.region_source: On 2026-04-20 the response for the val.run endpoint returned rndr-id and x-render-origin-server: Render, with Cloudflare in front (cf-ray). This indicates the request was served through Cloudflare's edge with a Render-backed origin. Val Town does not expose a region control to end users, so no single deployment region is declared by the platform.
providers.agent_native_score: poor — no CLI at time of deployment, code had to be pasted manually in the Val Town web editor

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Paste server code into the Val Town web editor
Click Run to publish — generates a live URL

providers.notes: At time of deployment (2026), Val Town had no CLI. The server code had to be pasted manually into the Val Town web editor. This blocks fully agent-native workflows. Val Town may have added CLI support since — to be re-verified.

providers.name: Render
providers.region_source: Render requires a single region to be chosen at service creation. The Render response headers do not expose the region (only rndr-id and x-render-origin-server: Render, with Cloudflare in front). The Render CLI requires interactive browser login and could not be queried non-interactively on 2026-04-20. The deployment region for this service needs to be confirmed from the Render dashboard.
providers.deployment_cli: none — deploys via GitHub repo connection
providers.free_tier_limit: Free web service sleeps after 15 minutes of inactivity. Cold start on next request.
providers.agent_native_score: partial — no CLI. Initial setup requires web interface (connect GitHub repo). Subsequent deployments are triggered by git push (agent can do this).

zero_to_live_sequence.action: Create a Render account (GitHub OAuth accepted)
Connect GitHub repo and configure build/start commands in the Render dashboard
git push to the connected branch — triggers automatic deployment

sources.content: Free tier: web services sleep after 15 minutes of inactivity
Onboarding: connect GitHub repo via dashboard, then automatic deploy on git push. No CC required.

providers.notes: Free tier instance sleeps after inactivity — latency measurements include cold start time. Relevant to the ping frequency analysis in the benchmark.

providers.name: Fly.io
providers.exclusion_reason: No permanent free tier. Fly.io offers a 2-hour or 7-day trial only, after which a credit card is required to run any app. A benchmark that requires paid infrastructure cannot be reproduced freely by the community.
providers.credit_card_required: yes — required to deploy any app beyond the 2h/7-day trial
providers.free_tier_limit: 2 hours of machine runtime or 7 days, whichever comes first
providers.agent_native_score: CLI excellent but CC is a hard blocker — requires human intervention to add payment before any deployment

sources.content: Free trial details: 2 hours of machine runtime or 7 days, whichever comes first. Apps stop running after the trial ends.
List of available regions and compute pricing

providers.notes: The fly CLI is one of the best available for agent-native deployments. The only blocker is the payment requirement. Fly.io would be ideal for pingers across multiple regions if cost were not a constraint.

providers.name: Google Cloud Run
providers.exclusion_reason: Used as the pinger platform for the benchmark. A provider cannot play both roles (pinger and benchmarked MCP server) — doing so would produce artificially low latency due to potential internal Google network routing. Google Cloud Run was chosen as the pinger platform because it is the only free, permanent solution covering all 7 required geographic regions including the Middle East.
providers.credit_card_required: yes — required to activate APIs (run, scheduler, build, artifact registry). Google blocks API activation without a billing account even when usage stays within the free tier. No charges are incurred as long as usage stays within the free tier limits.
providers.agent_native_deployment: yes — fully agent-native after initial setup. Full setup sequence for a developer starting from scratch: (1) create a Google account — browser, one-time; (2) install gcloud CLI — 429 MB on disk (macOS, measured 2026-04-16); (3) run 'gcloud auth login' — opens browser once for OAuth, then all subsequent commands run in the terminal; (4) add a credit card at console.cloud.google.com/billing — browser, one-time, required to activate APIs even within the free tier; (5) all remaining steps are CLI-only: project creation, API activation, image build and push, Cloud Run deployment, Cloud Scheduler setup — no browser interaction required.
providers.free_tier_limit: 2 million requests per month, 360,000 GiB-seconds RAM, 180,000 vCPU-seconds — shared across all regions within the same billing account. Resets monthly.
providers.agent_native_score: excellent for pinger use — permanent free tier, full CLI deployment via gcloud, 44 regions covering all 7 required continents including Middle East

sources.content: Free tier: 2M requests/month, 360,000 GiB-seconds RAM, 180,000 vCPU-seconds. Shared across all regions within a billing account. No time limit.
44 available regions across all continents including Middle East (Tel Aviv, Doha, Dammam)

providers.notes: Even though a credit card is required to create a GCP account, the free tier is permanent and generous enough to run multiple pingers across all regions without incurring costs. Estimated usage: ~12,000 requests/month for 12 pingers checking 8 providers every 6 hours (30-min relay) — well within the 2M free monthly limit.

providers.name: Azure Container Apps
providers.credit_card_required: yes — Azure always requires a credit card or debit card to create an account, even for the free tier
providers.free_tier_limit: Per subscription per calendar month: first 180,000 vCPU-seconds free, first 360,000 GiB-seconds free, first 2 million HTTP requests free. Resets monthly.
providers.agent_native_score: good for deployment — full CLI available. CC is a blocker for account creation but free tier is permanent once account exists.

sources.content: Free tier: 180,000 vCPU-seconds, 360,000 GiB-seconds, 2M HTTP requests per subscription per calendar month. No time limit — resets monthly.

providers.notes: Free tier is identical in volume to Google Cloud Run. CC required to create Azure account. Status is 'pending': Azure Container Apps has been evaluated on paper (free tier viable, CLI deployable) but no MCP server has been deployed to it yet, so it is not in the live latency measurement loop.

providers.name: Replit
providers.exclusion_reason: Free plan published apps go offline automatically after 30 days. A benchmark requires servers that stay up permanently. Keeping a Replit app running beyond 30 days requires a paid plan (Core at $18/month).
providers.credit_card_required: no for account creation, yes to keep a server running beyond 30 days
providers.deployment_cli: none — deploys via Replit web interface only
providers.free_tier_limit: 1 published app, automatically goes offline after 30 days. 2GB workspace storage. Daily Agent credits (monthly cap). Monthly credits for databases, object storage, AI integrations.
providers.agent_native_score: poor — no CLI, browser-only deployment, 30-day limit on free published apps

sources.content: Pricing comparison page — does NOT mention the 30-day limit on free published apps
Starter plan technical documentation — discloses that free published apps go offline after 30 days

providers.notes: WARNING: The 30-day limit is not mentioned anywhere on the pricing comparison page (https://replit.com/pricing). It is only disclosed in the technical documentation. A developer who deploys on the free plan will not discover this limit until their app goes offline.

providers.name: Alpic
providers.exclusion_reason: Alpic acts as an MCP gateway that requires the standard initialize handshake and an mcp-session-id header before tools/list is accepted. The benchmark contract measures tools/list as a single stateless POST, identical across all providers. Adapting the 12 pingers to a stateful MCP session flow would change the measurement for all providers and break comparability. Alpic is therefore excluded until the benchmark either adopts a stateful flow or Alpic supports stateless tools/list.
providers.credit_card_required: no — the deployment path tested did not request a credit card
providers.agent_native_score: excellent for deployment — dedicated CLI (alpic deploy), permanent free tier, full agent-native deployment verified. The blocker is measurement compatibility, not deployment.

sources.content: Free plan: $0/mo, 10,000 requests/month, 7-day analytics retention.
Alpic is a cloud platform for MCP-based AI apps. It supports one-click deploy, hosting, environments, and scaling for MCP servers.
Official Alpic MCP server template — uses skybridge framework and alpic deploy CLI
OpenAI MCP deployment guide — recommends Alpic as a hosting provider

providers.notes: Verified on 2026-04-20. A Node MCP server was deployed end-to-end by a coding agent with the alpic CLI, no credit card. During measurement attempts, Alpic rejected direct stateless tools/list requests with 'Bad Request: Server not initialized'. The correct flow (initialize → capture mcp-session-id → tools/list with that id) works, but it is not the flow used by the other 8 providers in this benchmark. The Alpic deployment created for this test was torn down to avoid leaving an orphan project.

providers.name: Cerebrium
providers.credit_card_required: no for signup; the public homepage advertises $30 free credit and no credit card required
providers.free_tier_limit: $30 free credit. The public pricing page also exposes a Hobby plan with free account creation and pay-as-you-use compute.
providers.agent_native_score: good — CLI deploy, no credit card advertised, but the free credit is finite

sources.content: $30 free credit - No credit card required. Deploy LLMs, agents and vision models globally.
Mistral recommends Cerebrium as a tool for infrastructure management; setup uses cerebrium login and cerebrium deploy.

providers.notes: The public docs advertise a finite free credit, not a permanent free allotment.

providers.name: Alibaba Cloud Function Compute
providers.credit_card_required: yes — the account creation flow shown in practice asks for a credit card before the console can be used
providers.free_tier_limit: First-time users can receive free trial quota for Function Compute across 12 monthly cycles.
providers.agent_native_score: excluded — the provider asks for an address and a credit card before the console can be used, so it does not fit the benchmark's no-card criterion

sources.content: Function Compute can host custom MCP services. The page documents template-based one-click deployment and remote MCP support.
First-time Function Compute users can claim free trial quota. The trial is granted for 12 monthly cycles.

providers.notes: This provider was evaluated and then excluded. The official offering is a time-limited free trial quota. In practice, account creation asks for an email address, a password, phone verification, a physical address, and a credit card before access to the console is usable. The account cancellation flow is only available in Chinese.

providers.name: Tencent CloudBase
providers.credit_card_required: unknown — the consulted CloudBase pages mention free trials but do not state the account payment requirement
providers.free_tier_limit: Free tier is granted every month during the first three months after SCF activation. It includes 1,000,000 event-triggered invocations, 1,000,000 HTTP-triggered invocations, 1,000,000 GB of resource usage, and 2 GB of outbound traffic per month.
providers.agent_native_score: good — the official CloudBase MCP page documents one-click deployment to Tencent Cloud development platform and remote SSE access

sources.content: CloudBase supports one-click deployment of MCP servers to Tencent Cloud development platform with remote SSE access.
CloudBase is a cloud-native integrated development platform and the product page says free trials are available for new users.
Serverless Cloud Function free tier is granted for the first three months after activation, then switches to a basic package tier.

providers.notes: This is the Tencent candidate that most directly matches MCP server deployment in the official docs. The free tier is time-limited: it lasts for the first three months after activation, then the service changes to a paid basic package tier unless usage is zero.
